title: Bread and Butter Pudding
subtitle: Classic baked custard pudding with buttered bread
excerpt: Sliced white bread, buttered and layered in a dish, soaked in a rich vanilla custard, and baked until the top crusts over while the interior remains soft and yielding. One of the best uses for slightly stale bread.
sourceNotes: • Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), §1304, bread-and-butter pudding recipe. Project Gutenberg #10136. Beeton calls for a custard of eggs, milk, and sugar with currants scattered between the layers; this version follows her proportions with the addition of vanilla.

content.text: Bread and butter pudding is the British answer to a dry loaf. You butter the slices on one side, layer them in a dish, pour over a custard of eggs, milk, cream, and sugar, and leave them to soak before baking. The top layer crusts to a golden, slightly crisp finish; everything below stays soft and custardy. A thirty-minute soak before baking is the part most versions skip, and it makes a real difference to the texture.

White bread is traditional and gives the lightest texture. Slightly stale bread absorbs the custard better than fresh. Brioche, challah, or pain de campagne all work well as richer variations. The custard ratio here is 3 eggs to 400 ml of liquid, enough to saturate six slices of medium bread without making the pudding waterlogged.

Butter each slice of bread on one side with {{unsalted-butter}} of softened butter. Cut each slice diagonally into two triangles. Arrange the triangles, buttered side up, in overlapping rows in a buttered rectangular baking dish (approximately 28 × 20 cm). Scatter {{sultanas}} of sultanas between the layers if using.

Whisk {{eggs}} eggs with {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar and {{vanilla-extract}} of vanilla extract in a bowl until combined. Warm {{whole-milk}} of whole milk and {{double-cream}} of double cream in a small saucepan to just below simmering point, the surface should shiver but not bubble. Pour the warm liquid over the egg mixture, whisking constantly. Pour this custard slowly and evenly over the bread. Press the bread down gently with a fork so each piece absorbs the liquid.

Leave the dish to soak for at least 30 minutes at room temperature (or up to 4 hours in the fridge). When ready to bake, heat the oven to 170°C fan. Scatter {{demerara-sugar}} of demerara sugar over the top. Bake for 30, 35 minutes until the custard is just set with a slight wobble in the centre and the top is golden and beginning to crisp. Rest for 10 minutes before serving.

items.fix: Bake at 170°C fan maximum. The custard should have a slight wobble in the centre when you remove it, it firms further as it rests.
items.cause: The oven was too hot, or the pudding baked too long

items.fix: Ensure the top triangles of bread point upward above the custard so they dry out and crisp in the oven. The exposed bread catches the heat.
items.cause: Too much custard, or the top layer was pushed below the surface
items.symptom: Bread is soggy and hasn't crisped on top

items.fix: Warm the milk to just below simmering, steam rising but no bubbles, then pour slowly while whisking constantly.
items.cause: The milk was too hot when poured onto the eggs, scrambling them

items.fix: Press the bread down gently with a fork every ten minutes during the soak, particularly any pieces that are resisting absorption.
items.cause: The bread was not pressed down into the custard during the soaking period
items.symptom: Bread floats and the layers separate

content.text: Brioche bread and butter pudding: substitute brioche slices for the white bread and add an extra egg yolk to the custard. The result is richer and more custardy. Marmalade bread and butter pudding: spread each slice with a thin layer of marmalade instead of plain butter before arranging in the dish; add the finely grated zest of an orange to the custard. For a croissant version, split day-old croissants lengthways and use them in place of the bread, the flaky layers crisp beautifully at the edges.

The pudding has Victorian roots, with currants, nutmeg, and a plain milk custard. It belongs to a long tradition of British bread-economy cooking: using stale bread before it goes to waste. The dish was in the national repertoire well before the Victorian era too. By the mid-twentieth century it had picked up an unfashionable reputation as the pudding of school dinners and wartime economy. That reputation has reversed. The dish now appears on restaurant menus as often as at home, often made with enriched bread and a custard generous enough to pool under the serving spoon.
